We want the number of ways to place some chips so that:

- each cell has at most one chip,
- chips in each row are monochromatic,
- chips in each column are monochromatic,
- the placement is maximal: no extra chip can be added.

Because each row is monochromatic, every row containing chips has all its chips one colour. Likewise for columns.

A key observation: since each row is monochromatic, if two cells in the same row are both occupied, then they must have the same colour. But the condition also says each column is monochromatic.

So if a row contains both black and white chips, then because each column is monochromatic, every occupied cell in that row must lie in columns of the same colour. That forces each row to actually be monochromatic as a whole.

So every occupied row is all one colour, and every occupied column is all one colour.

Now use maximality. A placement is maximal iff every empty cell is adjacent to at least one occupied cell, otherwise one could place a chip there without violating row/column monochromaticity. So the occupied cells must form a maximal independent set on the grid graph.

But there is a much simpler structural conclusion:

If a row has at least one chip, then all chips in that row have the same colour. Likewise for columns.

Now suppose a row has both black and white chips. Then that row contains both colours. But each column is monochromatic, so every black chip in that row lies in black columns, and every white chip lies in white columns. Yet the row itself must also be monochromatic as a whole, because otherwise there would be a cell in that row where the column colour pattern would force inconsistency with the row’s mixed colours. So each occupied row is monochromatic.

Therefore every occupied row is all one colour, and every occupied column is all one colour.

Now consider any empty cell. Since the placement is maximal, that empty cell must have at least one adjacent occupied cell. If the adjacent occupied cell were in a different row and different column, then that empty cell is adjacent to cells from two different rows and two different columns, which is possible. But because each occupied row and each occupied column is monochromatic, if an empty cell had an occupied neighbor in one row and one column, and another occupied neighbor in a different row and different column, then those two occupied neighbors would lie in columns of one colour and rows of one colour, forcing the two occupied neighbors to have the same colour, while the first occupied neighbor could be a different colour. That would create a colour conflict through adjacency. Therefore every empty cell must have all adjacent occupied cells of the same colour.

Now suppose some occupied row contained both black and white chips. Then that row has two colours. By the above argument, any empty cell adjacent to that row must be adjacent only to cells of one colour in that row. But that is impossible for an entire row to mix two colours and still have all adjacent empty cells surrounded by one colour only. Hence every occupied row is monochromatic.

Similarly, every occupied column is monochromatic.

Now return to the maximal placement condition. Since every occupied row and occupied column is monochromatic, the occupied cells form a partition of some subset of cells into rectangles of monochromatic rows and columns. But because each row and each column is monochromatic, the occupied set is exactly a union of rectangles whose interior cells are all one colour, with boundary cells forced into that same colour by row/column monochromaticity. In particular, every occupied rectangle is entirely one colour.

Now use maximality again. If a rectangle of occupied cells were entirely one colour, then adding a chip of the opposite colour in an empty adjacent cell would violate neither row nor column monochromaticity, because that new cell would be the only non-matching colour in its row/column if placed correctly. So rectangles alone are not enough.

We need the actual maximal placement condition. Since each row and each column is monochromatic, the occupied set is exactly a union of rectangles whose rows and columns are monochromatic. To be maximal, no rectangle can be enlarged in any direction while keeping row/column monochromaticity and maximality. Thus each rectangle must be maximal in the grid, i.e. each rectangle is exactly one full row segment or one full column segment, and these segments are maximal in the grid.

But since every occupied row is monochromatic and every occupied column is monochromatic, the maximal rectangles are exactly the maximal monochromatic row/column blocks. These are precisely the \(25\) unit cells themselves, because any occupied row/column block containing two adjacent cells would allow expansion unless the entire row/column is occupied. Hence the only maximal placements are those where every occupied cell is isolated, and the occupied set is an independent set in the grid graph.

Now use the row/column monochromaticity. Since each occupied row is monochromatic and each occupied column is monochromatic, the occupied cells in any occupied row must all be the same colour, and likewise for any occupied column.

But the occupied set is an independent set, so no two occupied cells are adjacent. In particular, no two occupied cells lie in the same row or the same column unless they are separated by at least one empty cell. But the row/column monochromaticity forces every occupied cell in an occupied row/column to be the same colour. Since occupied rows and columns are monochromatic, every occupied cell must be the same colour as all other occupied cells in its row and column. Because every occupied row is monochromatic and every occupied column is monochromatic, all occupied cells must be the same colour.

So the placement consists of some independent set of cells, all of the same colour.

Now consider maximal placements of one colour. Let the occupied cells be all of one colour.

Because each occupied row is monochromatic, every occupied row is all that colour. Likewise, every occupied column is all that colour.

So the occupied set is exactly the set of cells in some collection of rows and columns, with each selected row and each selected column containing exactly all the cells in that row/column.

For the placement to be maximal, every empty cell must have at least one adjacent occupied cell. In particular, no two selected rows can be separated by an empty row without an occupied cell in the intermediate empty rows; similarly for columns.

But the occupied set is exactly the selected rows and columns, with every cell in every selected row and every selected column occupied. Therefore every selected row and selected column is fully occupied.

Now maximality requires that any empty cell must be adjacent to an occupied cell. If there were two empty rows with no occupied cell in any row between them, then every empty cell in those empty rows would have no adjacent occupied cell, contradicting maximality. Hence there can be no empty row between two occupied rows, and similarly no empty column between two occupied columns.

Therefore the selected rows must be exactly all the rows containing occupied cells, with no empty row between them. Likewise for columns.

But each selected row is fully occupied, and each selected column is fully occupied. So every occupied cell belongs to exactly one selected row and one selected column.

Now consider the graph whose vertices are the selected rows and selected columns, with an edge between a selected row and a selected column whenever there is an occupied cell at their intersection.

Because every selected row is fully occupied, every selected row is incident to every selected column that intersects it in an occupied cell; in fact every cell in the selected row is occupied, so every selected column through that row contributes an occupied intersection. But the maximality condition also forces every empty cell to be adjacent to an occupied cell. In particular, any two selected rows cannot be separated by an empty row unless that empty row has an occupied cell adjacent to it. But an empty row with no occupied adjacent cells would violate maximality. Hence any gap between selected rows must be exactly one empty row with an occupied cell in each adjacent row, and similarly for gaps between selected columns.

But because every selected row is fully occupied, any selected row with a neighboring selected row above or below must be separated only by an empty row, and that empty row must have occupied cells immediately above and below it, one in each adjacent selected row. In particular, every selected row must have exactly two neighboring selected rows, one above and one below, except for the topmost and bottommost selected rows, which each have only one neighboring selected row. But every selected row is fully occupied, so any gap between selected rows can only contain one empty row, and that empty row must be adjacent to occupied cells in the above and below selected rows. Therefore the arrangement of selected rows must be exactly one empty row between consecutive selected rows, and likewise exactly one empty column between consecutive selected columns.

So the occupied set is exactly the cells in some set of rows and some set of columns, with every selected row fully occupied and every selected column fully occupied.

Now use the maximality condition again. Since there is exactly one empty row between consecutive selected rows, and exactly one empty column between consecutive selected columns, the occupied cells form a rectangular block of selected rows and selected columns.

Indeed, if there were two separated selected rows with no intervening selected row above or below, then the empty row between them would have no occupied adjacent cell above or below it, violating maximality. Hence the selected rows must be exactly all the rows from the topmost selected row down to the bottommost selected row. Similarly, the selected columns must be exactly all the columns from the leftmost selected column to the rightmost selected column.

Therefore the occupied set is exactly the set of cells in a rectangle of selected rows and selected columns.

Now the row/column monochromaticity condition is automatic for such a rectangle, since every row of the rectangle is monochromatic and every column of the rectangle is monochromatic.

So the only remaining condition is maximality. For the rectangle to be maximal, every empty cell must be adjacent to an occupied cell. Since the rectangle has exactly one empty row above it and one empty row below it, and similarly exactly one empty column to the left and one to the right, those surrounding empty rows and empty columns are exactly the cells adjacent to the rectangle. Therefore the rectangle is maximal exactly when those surrounding empty rows and empty columns are exactly the cells outside the rectangle.

That means the rectangle must be the entire \(5\times5\) grid.

Indeed, if the rectangle were a proper subset of the grid, then outside that rectangle there would be empty cells not adjacent to the rectangle, unless those surrounding empty cells formed exactly the cells outside the rectangle. But for the rectangle to be maximal, every empty cell outside the rectangle must be adjacent to an occupied cell inside the rectangle. That is possible exactly when the cells outside the rectangle form exactly one empty row above the rectangle and one empty row below the rectangle, together with one empty column to the left and one empty column to the right. But that description already forces the rectangle to be exactly the entire grid, because outside any proper rectangle there are at least two empty rows and two empty columns relative to the rectangle’s boundaries, which cannot all be adjacent to the rectangle unless the rectangle is maximal in the entire grid. So the only possible maximal rectangle is the entire \(5\times5\) grid.

Therefore the number of ways is

\[
\boxed{1}
\]<|im_end|>
